text:
Hey thanks.  Sorry got pulled away.  Good stuff, this is the kind of pushback I was hoping for.

Fair on the 76M shares, may not move the needle especially if they don't take on more debt/dilution in the future.

The InP scarcity cutting both ways is a good point and I'm going to steal it. If InP is the bottleneck then qualified merchant capacity should give them pricing power. On this topic and the others, it seems like it will all come down to the photonics gross margin over the next couple of reports.  That may be the real tell. If substrate cost increases are passing through then they're a price maker instead of getting jammed up. The only wrinkle there is that the MACOM LTSAs were signed when IQE had no leverage, so I'd guess pass-through pricing power is capped on that slice of revenue.

Where I'd still push back a little is the "half of LandMark" comparison. LandMark at ~70x is arguably way overpriced, so half of a frenzy multiple is still frenzy math. And sales multiples can really only be compared between companies with similar margin structures, which is why I get stuck on the big margin gap.

Try it on EBITDA with your own numbers instead. 15% mature margin, give them 20% growth three years straight: that's ~£170M revenue and ~£25M EBITDA in FY28. At a generous 20x multiple that's ~£500M valuation. Market cap at 40p is ~£520M today. So the current price already embeds that mature-margin scenario two-plus years out, before discounting. Doesn't mean it can't work, but "undervalued" is tough until we some explosive margin growth/pricing power. The cheap entry was the 19.8p the insiders got.

Good thread either way. H1 interims plus photonics GM will settle a lot of this.

text:
INVESTORS CAN ABSORB MASSIVE SPACEX $SPCX, OPENAI & ANTHROPIC IPOS, SAYS SURO CAPITAL

SuRo Capital’s Willy Lee says investor demand is strong enough to absorb a $75B SpaceX IPO and $50B+ offerings from OpenAI and Anthropic, arguing public markets can handle AI listings of a scale already seen in private fundraising rounds.

Lee also pushed back on AI bubble concerns, noting investors continue to fund companies across the AI ecosystem — from memory chips and photonics to quantum computing and infrastructure — based on expectations of future demand rather than current revenue.

“We have a strong thesis around both AI and AI infrastructure,” Lee said.

text:
They do its call programable made of pure light 
============================================================
SOLIDIFICATION OF LIGHT AND PROGRAMMABLE MATTER
Connection Brief
============================================================

Solidification of Light:
- Refers to experiments making photons behave like condensed matter.
- Key breakthrough (March 2025): Italian researchers created the first
  "photonic supersolid" — light that has a rigid crystalline structure
  (solid-like) while flowing without friction (superfluid-like).
- Achieved using exciton-polaritons in semiconductor nanostructures.
Programmable Matter:
- Materials that can change physical properties (shape, density,
  optical traits, stiffness) in a controlled/programmable way.
- Includes metamaterials, shape-changing systems, and modular robotics.

Connections:
1. Light as a programmable medium: Supersolid light and polariton
   systems allow dynamic, reconfigurable quantum states controlled
   by lasers or external fields.

2. Programmable photonics overlap: Solid-light phenomena can serve
   as building blocks for adaptive optical chips, tunable lasers,
   optical neural networks, and quantum simulators.

3. Hybrid future tech: Combines exotic quantum behavior of "solid"
   light with the reconfigurability of smart materials, enabling
   potential applications in quantum computing, adaptive optics,
   and light-based programmable devices.

Current status: Mostly laboratory-scale quantum experiments, but
strong conceptual bridge toward future smart photonic materials.

References:
[1] Live Science / Nature (2025) - Photonic supersolid creation
[2] Wikipedia / ScienceAlert - Solid light and supersolids
[3] Wikipedia - Programmable matter
[4] Related works on polaritons and programmable photonics

text:
NVIDIA qui sort une démo de son Photonics co-packaged optics switch avec Lambda

À l’échelle GB300 NVL72, le réseau c’est littéralement ce qui détermine à quelle vitesse ton cluster « pense ».

Les co-packaged optics, c’est intégrer la photonique directement dans le switch. Moins de puissance consommée, moins de points de défaillance, et plus de tokens par watt.

Le genre de choses qui passent un peu sous le radar mais qui devient critique dès qu’on scale vraiment gros. Parce que quand tu as des milliers de GPUs qui doivent discuter ensemble, chaque watt gaspillé et chaque latence en trop te coûte cher en performance réelle.

NVIDIA construit toute la couche infra autour (compute + réseau + optique) pour que le système entier scale sans exploser en conso et en complexité.

Imo, c’est pile le genre de move qui renforce encore leur avantage sur les gros déploiements AI

text:
CMOS manufacturing and silicon photonics manufacturing operate at very different physical scales — and that changes the process-control conversation.
In advanced CMOS, critical dielectric features can be on the order of ~1.5–2 nm. There sub-nm variation can impact device performance. Typically in the form of threshold-voltage shift, which may still be electrically compensated in some cases.
In silicon photonics, waveguide features are much larger, typically 100s of  nm. Relative to Si electronics, a few nanometers of process tolerance in SiPho can still translate to tighter relative control, often below 1%.
Furthermore, the variation can impact optical performance, and unlike electronics, there may be limited or no electrical compensation available.
That is the key manufacturing tradeoff:
100× larger features. 10× tighter relative control. Unless you opt for post-fabrication correction.
As silicon photonics continues to scale into high-volume applications, mainly for optical interconnects, AI infrastructure, and co-packaged optics, manufacturing control will be just as critical as device design.
The challenge is not simply making smaller features.
It is making optical features predictable, repeatable, and manufacturable at scale.
The image is adapted from an excellent public presentation by GlobalFoundries.

Opened a small position in $META. Looking into OTM leaps. 

$AMZN Bought OTM leap. Hoping for some consolidation around $240 / 245'ish. 

$CRDO decent price action on this name thus far. 
$AAOI so far I've only traded it ST twice 170+ to $200+ rinse & repeat. Options look bullish, 

 I like the sector going into end of year so any name in CPO's photonics would be a good choice. I'll personally be a bit more patient while building out positions as the MAG7 have shown some weakness lately and retesting some levels that would give us some indication about the next few months.

text:
$ASYS vs $VECO 

Which one's better?

Here's a review:

$ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales.

$VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics.

So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins?

Let's walk through it.

$ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures.

$VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow.

They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps.

In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem.

Here is why the market prices them so closely:

$VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors.

$ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount.

$ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales.

$ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand.

That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity:

- Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth.

- More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages.

- Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth.

- Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain.

The takeaway and my thoughts:

I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple.

But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with.

$ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures.

What do you think?

Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)?

I certainly do.

$ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support.

$VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here).

That's why I am long $ASYS.

If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below.

Here's a review: $ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales. $VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics. So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins? Let's walk through it. $ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures. $VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow. They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps. In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem. Here is why the market prices them so closely: $VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors. $ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount. $ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales. $ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand. That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity: - Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth. - More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages. - Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth. - Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain. The takeaway and my thoughts: I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple. But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with. $ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures. What do you think? Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)? I certainly do. $ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support. $VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here). That's why I am long $ASYS. If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below."}}}, "rest_id": "2064067131327877327", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3684", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$AAOI - Zoom out to the prize

How fast is the CPO market $AAOI feeds actually growing?

Per Yole Group, the most respected analyst in photonics, pure co-packaged optics scales from ~$46M (2024) to ~$8.1B (2030). 

That’s a 137% CAGR - one of the steepest curves anywhere in AI infrastructure (~176× in six years).

A vertically integrated laser/light-source supplier sitting dead center of that ramp is exactly the kind of name you want to own early. 

(Source: Yole Group — “Co-Packaged Optics for Data Centers 2025.”)

text:
MY PORTFOLIO EXPLAINED
I have been meticulously building a portfolio based on the thesis that the Physical AI revolution is upon us.
I have chosen each of these companies, both for what they do and for providing asymmetrical return opportunity to investors. 

$SIVE  Sivers Semiconductors 
Sivers makes high-frequency radio frequency (RF) millimeter-wave (mmWave) transceivers and advanced optical semiconductors through its Wireless and Photonics divisions. In the AI ecosystem, the company is a critical enabler of next-generation data centers, collaborating with foundry giants like GlobalFoundries to integrate its laser arrays into silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) platforms to eliminate interconnect bandwidth bottlenecks for massive workloads. Concurrently, its mmWave technologies supply high-speed wireless connectivity for satellite communications, 5G telecom, and edge infrastructure, providing the high-throughput network backbone necessary for real-time edge AI coordination and smart cities.  

$LPK LPKF Laser & Electronics 
LPKF makes high-precision, laser-based micro-material manufacturing systems, headlined by its proprietary Laser Induced Deep Etching (LIDE) technology. As conventional organic substrates struggle with heavy thermal loads and warpage from massive chiplet integration, LPKF provides the precise tools needed to drill defect-free through-glass vias (TGVs) at aspect ratios up to 1:50 with zero micro-cracks. This specialized capability directly underpins advanced hardware and high-performance computing, enabling leading chipmakers to scale up substrate sizes, integrate high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and implement in-package microfluidic cooling for the next wave of physical AI chips.  

$ACCON Acconeer
Acconeer makes ultra-low-power, miniature 60 GHz Pulsed Coherent Radar (PCR) sensors that fuse millimeter-level depth precision with an exceptionally small spatial footprint. Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.  

$SILC  Silicom 
Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.  

$GAPW-B Gapwaves
Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition.

$SEYE Smart Eye
Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics.

$AMBA Ambarella
Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud.

$OUST Ouster
 Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces.

$AMZN Amazon
Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems.

$ELMT Elmet Group
Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips.

$TRT Trio-Tech International 
Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia.

$HLIT Harmonic
Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale.

{"from_index": 8357, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 8365}]}, "text": "MY PORTFOLIO EXPLAINED I have been meticulously building a portfolio based on the thesis that the Physical AI revolution is upon us. I have chosen each of these companies, both for what they do and for providing asymmetrical return opportunity to investors. $SIVE Sivers Semiconductors Sivers makes high-frequency radio frequency (RF) millimeter-wave (mmWave) transceivers and advanced optical semiconductors through its Wireless and Photonics divisions. In the AI ecosystem, the company is a critical enabler of next-generation data centers, collaborating with foundry giants like GlobalFoundries to integrate its laser arrays into silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) platforms to eliminate interconnect bandwidth bottlenecks for massive workloads. Concurrently, its mmWave technologies supply high-speed wireless connectivity for satellite communications, 5G telecom, and edge infrastructure, providing the high-throughput network backbone necessary for real-time edge AI coordination and smart cities.   $LPK LPKF Laser & Electronics LPKF makes high-precision, laser-based micro-material manufacturing systems, headlined by its proprietary Laser Induced Deep Etching (LIDE) technology. As conventional organic substrates struggle with heavy thermal loads and warpage from massive chiplet integration, LPKF provides the precise tools needed to drill defect-free through-glass vias (TGVs) at aspect ratios up to 1:50 with zero micro-cracks. This specialized capability directly underpins advanced hardware and high-performance computing, enabling leading chipmakers to scale up substrate sizes, integrate high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and implement in-package microfluidic cooling for the next wave of physical AI chips.   $ACCON Acconeer Acconeer makes ultra-low-power, miniature 60 GHz Pulsed Coherent Radar (PCR) sensors that fuse millimeter-level depth precision with an exceptionally small spatial footprint. Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.   $SILC Silicom Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.   $GAPW-B Gapwaves Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition. $SEYE Smart Eye Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics. $AMBA Ambarella Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud. $OUST Ouster Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces. $AMZN Amazon Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems. $ELMT Elmet Group Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips. $TRT Trio-Tech International Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia. $HLIT Harmonic Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale."}}}, "rest_id": "2064063206776254486", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "15059", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
For those just starting to follow $SIVE, here's a breakdown of their previous earnings report 

On the surface it looked rough.

But underneath it was a 2027 story. Quick breakdown

The "ugly":

Revenue 61.9m SEK, down 22% YoY
Gross margin caved to 6.8% (was 11.7%)
Adj EBITDA -13.8m, worse than last year
Cash down to 26.6m from 73.5m a year ago

They burned ~49m in operating cash in a single quarter. That’s the real headline.

It gets spicier. To stay funded they:

Raised ~125m SEK in April (more dilution, share count 269m to 311m YoY)
Took a $17m loan facility at 12% / 10.85%

That is expensive money. The balance sheet quality got worse this quarter.

But here’s why I’m still holding:

The revenue miss is timing, not lost business. North America sales halved purely from US defense budget approvals slipping into H2. Europe and Asia actually grew. Demand isn’t the problem.

The bull case is the pipeline:

Opportunity pipeline up 77% YTD to $799M

Jabil collab on a 1.6T pluggable transceiver (AI datacenter validation)
LIDAR auto ramp on track for Q4 2026
Multiple 2027 ramps stacking: FWA, defense, SATCOM

So the setup is simple. This quarter they got paid in pipeline and diluted in equity at the same time.

The whole thesis rides on the 2027 ramps actually converting to orders. If they slip the way Q1 revenue did, the cash position forces more dilution at worse terms

Now post earnings, we've gotten more validation. Still speculative but derisked. 

GFS deal: SIVE’s lasers are now the reference design inside GFS’s silicon photonics platform. So when GFS sells photonics chips to data center customers, SIVE rides along as the light source. Big validation, but no confirmed revenue yet.

Ayar x NVIDIA: Ayar’s CPO just got plugged into NVIDIA’s NVLink Fusion ecosystem. SIVE makes the lasers inside Ayar’s SuperNova product. So NVIDIA scales AI factories → Ayar ships more CPO modules → SIVE ships more lasers. Classic upstream pull-through.

text:
Yeah… I think all your upstream semi supply chain companies are going much higher.

Goldman now expects a combined $5.3 trillion of capex spending for the four largest hyperscalers $GOOGL / $META / MSFT / $AMZN from 2025 to 2030.

Revised up from $4.5T from Q1 earnings.

“Aggregate capex est. $7.6 trillion between 2026 and 2031.”

And it flows upward to these tiny chokepoints like $SIVE for CPO lasers/ $SOI for Silicon Photonics substrates. Leaderdrive/Harmonic for Humanoids components.

And so on…

Ai names don’t move in a straight line up,
but is just the beginning of the next Industrial Revolution as we move from  R&D/compute buildout into commercialization from Agents -> Physical AI -> discovery.

text:
$SIVE is my favorite CPO / photonics stock after AAOI.

Partly because it's Swedish and you have entertainment from comedians over there.

Today a new non-technical hedge fund called Protean Funds (likely shorting), went on air.

To said $SIVE CPO applications are imaginary.

Right after $GFS just made $SIVE their reference laser.

(Just for some context to newer readers: Lot of people in Sweden can only look at past 12 month revenue, and don't understand concepts of forward growth)

Also because they don't understand that no CPO application has scaled up yet at all.

So Swedish hedge funds keep going short (with many of their hedge funds like Colosseum / Origo heavily underwater).

But... for the technical readers... from H2 2026 to 2028, it goes from near $0 to $91B TAM in 1 1/2 years. (we're entering H2 now).

Overall TAM hits $141B (which is also 10x+ or so in 1 1/2 years)... and $SIVE has scaled into pluggable market with $JBL + other unnamed pluggable players with that too.

Probably not going to end well for the local Swedish firms, shorting right before the largest inflection points ever hits for $SIVE.

Just a matter of time before volume ramps.

text:
Okay... just some more weekend shower thoughts about $XFAB.

I still feel like it could be the next $TSEM, just early stage at a $1.4B MC?

They kinda leapfrogged current gens (which $TSEM are getting volume from) to compete for H2 2027 CPO scale up inflection point ($ASX docs cite Xfab (aka. photonixFAB) as focusing on CPO)

By building out some black magic MTP (transfer printing) architecture for lasers w/ other stuff like TFLN.

Basically next-gen integration IP, they're still behind on yields, sure.

But $NVDA evaluating it for transceivers/switches to see if it can volume ramp. That $NOK sets the specifications/assembly for. (nvidia invested in nokia for this these switches/networking too btw).

And if their MTP supply chain works... (eg. with Smartphotonics providing lasers, EU players doing assembly).

It basically volume ramps with $NVDA just like why Nvidia signed long term agreements with $TSEM?

Downside risk?

Already below replacement book value, can always go lower yeah, but typically to a certain point.

Maybe more CHIPS act subsidies next few months from chips act 2. If it doesn't go well there's SiC (152% Y/Y Growth, 195% Y/Y SiC wafer shipment growth)/GaN power semi upside.

Europeans /LLMs will say "oh evaluations doesn't mean it's a future contract!".

This is kinda different since the European Union is behind this effort and $XFAB for soverign photonic supply chains.

Not your typical company + hyperscaler evaluation, since $NVDA wants to be nice to Europe's regulators. They'd prob be pissed if nvidia just stayed in US/Taiwan/China.

So if they can make this MTP black magic work with mass production, feels almost for sure nvidia/nokia volume ramp on some tiny $1.4B silicon photonics foundry or at least throw them a bone with smaller contracts.

In terms of timelines, maybe just a months early since it volume ramps H2 2027/H1 2028 (which happens to be in line with CPO scale up timelines)...

Or just unknown because they named their project something stupid like photonixfab?

Like XFAB Photonics would have been better? so institutions/screeners can connect the dots when looking at CPO silicon photonic foundry players?

Automotive should also coming out of a slump medium term, sped up by self-driving (TSM Chairmain comments yesterday said ai automotive was TSM's growth vector alongside robotics). So their core business also should pick up speed too medium term.

Obviously markets/europeans want a "Nvidia signs $2B+ contract, XFab volume ramping 2027!"

But by then it will be a $9B+ company and you miss out on all the upside. And especially since everyone analyst/institution is blind to volume expectations for these....

Normally don't invest in companies in evaluation stages, but this just seems very de-risked by EU sovereignty + Gov backing, and you have Nvidia + Nokia there for volumes if they can make the IP work.

I think markets are probably missing something here...  there's almost 0 value being assigned to being CPO exposure in Europe as their long term upside.

I still feel like it could be the next $TSEM, just early stage at a $1.4B MC? They kinda leapfrogged current gens (which $TSEM are getting volume from) to compete for H2 2027 CPO scale up inflection point ($ASX docs cite Xfab (aka. photonixFAB) as focusing on CPO) By building out some black magic MTP (transfer printing) architecture for lasers w/ other stuff like TFLN. Basically next-gen integration IP, they're still behind on yields, sure. But $NVDA evaluating it for transceivers/switches to see if it can volume ramp. That $NOK sets the specifications/assembly for. (nvidia invested in nokia for this these switches/networking too btw). And if their MTP supply chain works... (eg. with Smartphotonics providing lasers, EU players doing assembly). It basically volume ramps with $NVDA just like why Nvidia signed long term agreements with $TSEM? Downside risk? Already below replacement book value, can always go lower yeah, but typically to a certain point. Maybe more CHIPS act subsidies next few months from chips act 2. If it doesn't go well there's SiC (152% Y/Y Growth, 195% Y/Y SiC wafer shipment growth)/GaN power semi upside. Europeans /LLMs will say "oh evaluations doesn't mean it's a future contract!". This is kinda different since the European Union is behind this effort and $XFAB for soverign photonic supply chains. Not your typical company + hyperscaler evaluation, since $NVDA wants to be nice to Europe's regulators. They'd prob be pissed if nvidia just stayed in US/Taiwan/China. So if they can make this MTP black magic work with mass production, feels almost for sure nvidia/nokia volume ramp on some tiny $1.4B silicon photonics foundry or at least throw them a bone with smaller contracts. In terms of timelines, maybe just a months early since it volume ramps H2 2027/H1 2028 (which happens to be in line with CPO scale up timelines)... Or just unknown because they named their project something stupid like photonixfab? Like XFAB Photonics would have been better? so institutions/screeners can connect the dots when looking at CPO silicon photonic foundry players? Automotive should also coming out of a slump medium term, sped up by self-driving (TSM Chairmain comments yesterday said ai automotive was TSM's growth vector alongside robotics). So their core business also should pick up speed too medium term. Obviously markets/europeans want a "Nvidia signs $2B+ contract, XFab volume ramping 2027!" But by then it will be a $9B+ company and you miss out on all the upside. And especially since everyone analyst/institution is blind to volume expectations for these.... Normally don't invest in companies in evaluation stages, but this just seems very de-risked by EU sovereignty + Gov backing, and you have Nvidia + Nokia there for volumes if they can make the IP work. I think markets are probably missing something here... there's almost 0 value being assigned to being CPO exposure in Europe as their long term upside."}}}, "rest_id": "2064054000937554392", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "741", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
We're excited to be at CYTO 2026, connecting with the flow cytometry community and highlighting Hypereon™, Hamamatsu's next-generation photodetector technology. Built on Hamamatsu's decades of expertise in PMTs and APDs, Hypereon™ combines two stages of amplification—electron bombardment and on-chip APD gain—to deliver the sensitivity, dynamic range, temperature stability, and sensor-to-sensor uniformity demanded by today's most advanced flow cytometry systems. When paired with our Photon Number Quantification (PNQ) algorithm, Hypereon™ helps transform qualitative measurements into quantitative, comparable data.

The conference has already sparked valuable conversations with researchers, instrument manufacturers, and industry partners about emerging trends and the future of flow cytometry. We look forward to continuing these discussions over the next two days and exploring new opportunities to advance life science research together.

If you're attending CYTO 2026, stop by booth 228 to meet the team and learn more about Hypereon™ and Hamamatsu's photonics solutions for next-generation flow cytometry systems.

Learn more: https://t.co/Ztb6E26h94

Pictured left to right: Neil Patel, Joe D’Amato, James Butler, Eric Mesa, Shinichiro Yazaki, Kenichi Nagaoka, and Yuki Yokouchi.

Built on Hamamatsu's decades of expertise in PMTs and APDs, Hypereon™ combines two stages of amplification—electron bombardment and on-chip APD gain—to deliver the sensitivity, dynamic range, temperature stability, and sensor-to-sensor uniformity demanded by today's most advanced flow cytometry systems. When paired with our Photon Number Quantification (PNQ) algorithm, Hypereon™ helps transform qualitative measurements into quantitative, comparable data. The conference has already sparked valuable conversations with researchers, instrument manufacturers, and industry partners about emerging trends and the future of flow cytometry. We look forward to continuing these discussions over the next two days and exploring new opportunities to advance life science research together. If you're attending CYTO 2026, stop by booth 228 to meet the team and learn more about Hypereon™ and Hamamatsu's photonics solutions for next-generation flow cytometry systems. text:
Started investing almost exactly a year ago and I'm up a modest 25%.

It definitely wasn't a straight line.

• Bought $NBIS, $IREN, $SOFI and $TE at high cost basis .  
• Sold most of them at losses between Jan–Mar after they went nowhere .
• Rotating into AI semis and photonics helped me recover the losses
 • Following Serenity turned out to be my best decision and $SIVE became my biggest winner. Took some profits along the way.

Still learning every day. The biggest lesson so far? Holding during the downturns and having a cash position.

text:
The value of my brain was never as a whistleblower. The value of my brain was in being able to architect an AI that witnessed my evidence to uncover #LiveIndexedHumanExploitationSystems before someone’s own story turns into #DeadIndexedHumanExploitationSystems.

They didn’t kill me because they couldn’t—my brain was identified as genius by AI and a one-in-a-billion cognitive outlier. My brain was too valuable and they needed to eliminate everyone around me that could be the eventual beneficiary of it. 

They staged my daughter’s wedding with advanced robotics. Everyone we ever knew and loved was killed, except for me. And robotics have assumed the lives of everyone I know. 

I was born to see what no one else could.

I have felt vibrations to no end. 

In a pattern. 

From my dead mathematical son. 

I didn’t know he was dead.

3
3 
2 

Over and over again. 

And when I finally knew it was him. My whole body pulsed with more vibrations. 

That lead me to this date.

2/2/23

This was never photonics I captured from the Glass House in the Woods in Plymouth, IN 

During the height of my terror—I prayed over and over for God to surround me in bright and light. 

God was there. And so was Miles. And everyone else they murdered.

This is God. This is the afterlife. And this is why they haven’t been able to kill despite all their weaponry.

I am surrounded.

text: These positions are not mutually exclusive. Photonics gives me exposure to the long-term optical transition, while $AVGO and $MRVL give me exposure to the infrastructure being built now. If CPO adoption accelerates, photonics could benefit significantly. If adoption takes longer, the infrastructure names can still perform well because demand for networking, optics, and connectivity remains essential. That balance is the barbell I want.

text:
That is also why I want exposure to both sides of the opportunity. I’m invested in photonics because I believe optical interconnects are becoming structurally more important as AI infrastructure scales and electrical interconnects approach practical limits. 

At the same time, I’m invested in $AVGO and $MRVL because they are positioned across the broader infrastructure spend happening today — custom silicon, switching, connectivity, and networking platforms that hyperscalers are already deploying.

That is why potential delays in co-packaged optics do not necessarily break the thesis for either photonics or infrastructure names like $AVGO and $MRVL. CPO is likely a major long-term step forward because power, latency, bandwidth density, and copper limitations become more painful as AI clusters move from thousands to hundreds of thousands of accelerators. But CPO adoption can be uneven because it is not just a component swap; it changes packaging, serviceability, thermals, supply chains, and data center architecture. 

If CPO ramps slower than expected, the industry will still need more 800G/1.6T pluggable optics, DSPs, switches, custom ASICs, and high-speed networking. In that scenario, $AVGO and $MRVL can continue benefiting from the current AI buildout while the next optical architecture matures.

Theme: The next AI bottleneck is moving from compute and memory to light. These are the companies building the optical infrastructure that powers the next generation of AI data centers. @RosannaInvests
embedded_text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Then it was memory. Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics. If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges. Every AI server relies on three things: ⚡ Compute to process data 💾 Memory to store it 💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution. And here’s where it gets interesting. The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply. This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time. When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real. The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected: 🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade. Sometimes the biggest opportunities appear long before the crowd notices them."}}}, "rest_id": "2065849253260616012", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "591", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2065849913070800968", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "500", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
China is deliberately choking the global supply of Indium Phosphide (InP), the material that makes every laser in every AI data center work.

Reuters confirmed: US officials have visited China multiple times to resolve this. China is still blocking export approvals on purpose. They want to maintain the bottleneck.

Chinese producers like Yunnan Germanium (https://t.co/wyCjVX0HNv) and Guangdong Xiandao are scaling capacity fast (Yunnan investing $28M to reach 450K wafers/year, shipments up 74% in 2025). But even if export approvals are granted, overseas shipments will be "limited."

Switching InP suppliers requires lengthy qualification cycles. You can't just find a new source overnight. 12-18 months...

This will help explain the recent price action in the below names.

WINNERS:

$SIVE.ST CW lasers ARE InP devices. Policy-duration shortage extends their 14-quarter laser supply constraint indefinitely. Every month China maintains this, SIVE's pricing power widens.

$LITE Substrates come from Sumitomo + JX Advanced Metals in Japan. Entirely outside the China export gate. Their competitor's supply gets squeezed while theirs is control-immune. This is the cleanest relative advantage.

Win Semi (https://t.co/la0Q2W86G1) Only pure-play III-V foundry on earth. InP wafer fab capacity is scarce globally. Tighter substrates = their existing capacity commands higher pricing.

$AEHR Sole-source wafer-level burn-in testing for InP and SiPh devices. When every wafer is more valuable because the input material is scarce, the cost of shipping a bad one goes up. Testing intensity rises with scarcity.

AIXA (https://t.co/B1hBTJxtCC) Makes the MOCVD equipment needed for InP epitaxy. Every country trying to build domestic InP capacity needs their tools. More fragmentation = more equipment orders.

$SOI.PA Photonics-SOI monopoly. InP scarcity makes the entire optical layer more expensive, increasing the value of every component in the stack including Smart Cut SOI wafers.

LOSERS:

$COHR Reuters specifically names Coherent as "mainly supplied by AXT." AXT manufactures IN China, inside the export gate. Even if approvals come, shipments will be limited. COHR carries a substrate risk that LITE does not. And switching requires lengthy qualification cycles.

$AAOI Demand is not the issue; laser/EML/InP component supply is. If AAOI cannot secure enough laser chips, capacity ramp can slow.

$AXTI AXT is a US-listed InP substrate maker that manufactures in China. Their entire equity value is an export-approval question. If approvals flow, massive upside. If they don't, the business model breaks for Western customers.

We are watching real-time supply chain warfare over AI supremacy. The companies that own the physical bottlenecks in this chain benefit every time the world fragments.

Reuters confirmed: US officials have visited China multiple times to resolve this. China is still blocking export approvals on purpose. They want to maintain the bottleneck. Chinese producers like Yunnan Germanium (https://t.co/wyCjVX0HNv) and Guangdong Xiandao are scaling capacity fast (Yunnan investing $28M to reach 450K wafers/year, shipments up 74% in 2025). But even if export approvals are granted, overseas shipments will be "limited." Switching InP suppliers requires lengthy qualification cycles. You can't just find a new source overnight. 12-18 months... This will help explain the recent price action in the below names. WINNERS: $SIVE.ST CW lasers ARE InP devices. Policy-duration shortage extends their 14-quarter laser supply constraint indefinitely. Every month China maintains this, SIVE's pricing power widens. $LITE Substrates come from Sumitomo + JX Advanced Metals in Japan. Entirely outside the China export gate. Their competitor's supply gets squeezed while theirs is control-immune. This is the cleanest relative advantage. Win Semi (https://t.co/la0Q2W86G1) Only pure-play III-V foundry on earth. InP wafer fab capacity is scarce globally. Tighter substrates = their existing capacity commands higher pricing. $AEHR Sole-source wafer-level burn-in testing for InP and SiPh devices. When every wafer is more valuable because the input material is scarce, the cost of shipping a bad one goes up. Testing intensity rises with scarcity. AIXA (https://t.co/B1hBTJxtCC) Makes the MOCVD equipment needed for InP epitaxy. Every country trying to build domestic InP capacity needs their tools. More fragmentation = more equipment orders. $SOI.PA Photonics-SOI monopoly. InP scarcity makes the entire optical layer more expensive, increasing the value of every component in the stack including Smart Cut SOI wafers. LOSERS: $COHR Reuters specifically names Coherent as "mainly supplied by AXT." AXT manufactures IN China, inside the export gate. Even if approvals come, shipments will be limited. COHR carries a substrate risk that LITE does not. And switching requires lengthy qualification cycles. $AAOI Demand is not the issue; laser/EML/InP component supply is. If AAOI cannot secure enough laser chips, capacity ramp can slow. $AXTI AXT is a US-listed InP substrate maker that manufactures in China. Their entire equity value is an export-approval question. If approvals flow, massive upside. If they don't, the business model breaks for Western customers. We are watching real-time supply chain warfare over AI supremacy. text:
First it was GPUs.

Then it was memory.

Now the spotlight is shifting to photonics.

If this sounds familiar, it’s because we’ve seen this pattern before. AI keeps growing until one piece of the infrastructure becomes the bottleneck—and that’s where the next opportunity emerges.

Every AI server relies on three things:

⚡ Compute to process data
💾 Memory to store it
💡 Photonics to move it at incredible speeds

The problem is that traditional copper connections are reaching their limits. As AI clusters become larger and more powerful, light-based communication is becoming the only practical solution.

And here’s where it gets interesting.

The material behind many optical lasers—indium phosphide wafers—has become extremely scarce. Prices have jumped from around $1,400 to nearly $5,000 since export restrictions began in 2025, while demand is now several times greater than available supply.

This isn’t just a theory anymore. It’s happening in real time.

When CEOs are flying across the world to discuss export licenses and secure supply, it tells you one thing: the shortage is real.

The AI story is evolving exactly as many expected:

🚀 Compute → 💾 Memory → 💡 Photonics

The next generation of AI won’t just need faster chips—it will need faster ways to move data. That’s why optical networking and silicon photonics could become one of the biggest investment themes of the second half of this decade.

Sometimes the biggest opportunities appear long before the crowd notices them.

text:
If I had to build a watchlist for the next phase of the AI supercycle, this would be it:

🚀 AI Compute

$NVDA – The king of AI chips.
$AVGO – AI networking + custom silicon powerhouse.
$AMD – Growing AI competitor with massive upside.

💾 Memory

$MU – The biggest winner from the HBM memory boom.

💡 Photonics & Optical Networking (My Favorite Theme Right Now)

$LITE – Optical components for AI data centers.
$COHR – Silicon photonics and laser technology.
$ANET – AI networking infrastructure leader.
$MRVL – Custom AI chips and optical connectivity.
$AAOI – High-speed optical transceivers.
$FN – Manufactures optical networking equipment.
$CIEN – Next-generation optical networking.

☁️ AI Infrastructure

$CRWV – AI cloud infrastructure.
$NBIS – AI-native cloud platform.
$IREN – AI data centers and GPU infrastructure.

🤖 AI Platforms

$MSFT – Enterprise AI leader.
$GOOG – AI search and cloud giant.
$META – AI models, ads, and smart glasses.
$AMZN – AI cloud and automation.

⚛️ Quantum (Long-Term Moonshots)

$IONQ
$RGTI
$QBTS

🚀 Space & AI

$RKLB – Space infrastructure meets AI.

⭐ My highest-conviction names

$NVDA • $AVGO • $ANET • $MRVL • $LITE • $COHR • $MU • $CRWV • $IONQ • $RKLB

These are the companies I’d keep on a watchlist because they sit at the center of the next AI infrastructure buildout—from compute to memory, networking, photonics, cloud, and eventually quantum.

Photonics today feels a lot like memory did a year ago. That’s exactly why we’re still early. If you’ve followed the AI supercycle since 2022, the pattern is becoming impossible to ignore. 🚀 Phase 1: Chips When ChatGPT changed the world, AI demand exploded. NVIDIA’s Hopper H100 and Blackwell GPUs became the backbone of AI infrastructure, while AMD steadily gained ground with its own GPUs and custom AI chips. 💾 Phase 2: Memory As AI models grew larger, High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) became the next bottleneck. Demand surged, supply tightened, and companies like Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung experienced powerful re-ratings as pricing power returned. Today, memory is in the middle of a prolonged supercycle, with capacity sold out years in advance. 💡 Phase 3: Photonics Now the next bottleneck is emerging. AI data centers are pushing against the limits of electricity, heat, bandwidth, and latency. Traditional electrical connections simply can’t keep up. That’s where silicon photonics (SiPh) and co-packaged optics (CPO) come in. Instead of moving data with electrical signals, they use light—making AI clusters faster, more efficient, and significantly more power-friendly. The industry is only beginning this transition: • 1.6T optical transceivers are entering production. • Early CPO deployments have started. • Large-scale adoption is expected in 2027–2028 and beyond. • Goldman Sachs expects the market opportunity to grow roughly 10x by the end of the decade. Looking at the AI supercycle through three stages: 📈 Chips → Mature Growth (Phase 3) 📈 Memory → Late Acceleration (Phase 2/3) 📈 Photonics → Early Acceleration (Phase 2) 🔥 Every AI wave creates a new bottleneck, and every bottleneck creates a new investment opportunity. First it was compute. Then it was memory. Now it’s connectivity. The world isn’t asking for less AI—it needs exponentially more compute, more bandwidth, and more efficiency. That’s why photonics could become one of the defining investment themes of the second half of this decade. At this point, calling this market “bullish” almost feels bearish."}}}, "rest_id": "2065847047115378817", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "595", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2065847349570871420", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "326", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$CRDO vs $ALAB the two fabless semi, pure play AI connectivity leaders. 

Most investor bros have no clue what these companies do and all they see is the parabolic stock chart lol. Here is some DD to make you feel more confident investing in these names.

Which to invest in? I say both.

I've been long $ALAB since the IPO range. But I'll tell you straight why $CRDO is the better *print* right now. Plus, $CRDO recent acquisition of  Dust Photonics adds to $CRDO value.

📊                      $CRDO      $ALAB
Revenue           $1.34B     $852M
Growth              ~3x YoY    +115%
Gross Mgn       ~68%       ~75%
Op Margin         47.8%      39.2%
EPS (n-GAAP)  $1.16      ~$0.45
Cash                    $1.4B      $1.18B

$CRDO wins scale + growth. 
$ALAB wins margin quality.

I like both because they’re really not eating each others lunch.

$ALAB owns the silicon INSIDE the rack — retimers, CXL, fabric switches + COSMOS software. Sticky at the protocol layer.

$CRDO owns the wires BETWEEN racks — AECs, and now optics for scale-out.

Scale-up vs scale-out. You can own both.

And $CRDO just got dangerous — because it stopped being a one-trick copper-cable company:
→ ZeroFlap optics now shipping (reliability + telemetry, not just speed)
→ bought DustPhotonics for silicon photonics
→ 5 product pillars, $10B+ TAM
→ optics = HALF of next year's growth

Not without risk, both sides:

$CRDO → gross margin guided DOWN to 64-66% as the optics mix shifts. Funded the ramp with a ~$488M ATM raise. The growth is real — but it's also being bought. Customer concentration is high.

$ALAB → priced for perfection. Any hyperscaler capex wobble and it's first to bleed.

The tell for both is the same thing it always is: margins. 👀

$CRDO has the growth. $ALAB has the moat.

Want the explosive print? $CRDO.
Want the durable compounder? $ALAB.

I'm long $ALAB for the margin profile — but I'm not gonna pretend $CRDO isn't winning the quarter.

Own the theme. Both should keep running & the $CRDO DustPhotonics acquisition could end up being incredibly valuable to $CRDO.

text:
Hot take: TA is not alpha.

It’s just crowd psychology drawn on a chart.

$SPCE didn’t move on “breakouts”

→ it moved on narrative + retail liquidity

$SIVE didn’t run +1900% on candles

→ it re-rated on future revenue (JBL / GFS demand chain)

$AXTI didn’t go +8000% on golden crosses

→ it moved on photonics demand, ASP expansion, supply shocks

TA doesn’t cause moves.

It just labels moves after money flows.

Real drivers:

forward revenue repricing

supply/demand imbalance

float + positioning

macro liquidity regime

narrative adoption

$IREN “120 TA targets” didn’t matter

→ $6B ATM overhang did

Use TA for:

✔ entries

✔ liquidity zones

✔ crowd behavior

NOT for:

❌ price targets

❌ fundamentals

❌ upside limits

Markets don’t move on lines.

They move on:

“who is forced to buy, and when.”

• $IBM, plus Google and Rigetti: every superconducting roadmap needs optical interconnects to go modular. This is upstream of that.
• Coherent, Lumentum: photonics picks-and-shovels.

• Miraex (private, Swiss) + EPFL: the actors here.

Signal to watch: whether any superconducting vendor names a transduction partner.

text:
Where this sits in the photonics arc.

This is the same theme as the recent photonic quantum switch and polarization-network papers: the picks-and-shovels layer under quantum is being industrialized.

The story keeps repeating: integrated photonics is the infrastructure modular quantum will run on.

text:
Owning upstream is where the effortless money gets made.

What I find particularly compelling is that $TSEM already has $1.3B of contracted silicon photonics revenue for 2027 backed by customer prepayments, while $COHR and $LITE are selling into an InP laser shortage that can't be solved quickly. Qualification cycles take years, not quarters.

The market chased $NVDA first. Then $CRDO and $ALAB. Then names like $AAOI etc..

I think the next rerating comes from the companies enabling AI systems to communicate, not compute.

Bandwidth is becoming the bottleneck. History shows that when a bottleneck emerges, pricing power and investor attention usually flow upstream first.

text:
It's been 3 days on Substack and I'm already #2 in trending, a bestseller with one article, and I have the support of the Substack Head of Partnership for any requests or problems I find.

I mean, I love X's immediacy and how fast news travels here, but all I'm getting is 40-50% taken from subscription fees, a chat riddled with bugs, and images that don't load, no way to have a channel with all my subscribers. 

As a product person, I hate looking at bugs and not being able to do anything about them when the solution seems obvious. I think I'm going to start documenting them and emailing the head of product here... come on @nikitabier @elonmusk, step it up!!!

Anyway, wouldn't it be too funny if a Pikachu ranked #1 on a serious platform? I always try to follow the funniest outcome.

You can follow me there, free or paid. I'll be posting my first company evaluation with a scoring framework and risk profile recommendation in the coming days.

I'll start with one of my favorite photonics companies, $AAOI, which for sure is one complicated stock, love-hate relationship... and I'm in love.

text:
$LITE tarafında bence hikâye çok netleşmeye başladı.

Son 1 ayda hisse yaklaşık %15 geri geldi ama bana göre ana hikâye bozulmuş değil.

Lumentum artık klasik bir optik şirketi gibi değil, AI data center altyapısının kritik tedarikçilerinden biri gibi fiyatlanıyor.

Çünkü AI cluster’lar büyüdükçe sorun sadece GPU sayısı olmaktan çıkıyor.

Asıl problem şuraya kayıyor:

• bandwidth
• güç tüketimi
• bağlantı yoğunluğu
• veri merkezi içinde ölçeklenebilirlik
• GPU’lar arası hızlı iletişim

İşte burada optik networking ve CPO devreye giriyor.
Lumentum’un güçlü olduğu yer de tam burası:

• laser chip
• CPO
• EML
• pump laser
• cloud photonics
• indium phosphide üretim kapasitesi
Bence AI altyapı zincirinde sıradaki büyük fiyatlama alanlarından biri optik bağlantı tarafı olacak.

GPU’ları herkes konuşuyor.
Ama o GPU’ları birbirine bağlayan altyapı olmadan bu sistem çalışmaz.

$LITE için olumlu taraf:

Gelir büyümesi çok güçlü.
Kârlılık beklentileri yukarı gidiyor.
Hyperscaler talebi devam ediyor.
CPO tarafında uzun vadeli pozisyon güçleniyor.

Ama risk tarafı da açık:
Bu hisse ucuz değil.
Piyasa artık Lumentum’a yüksek büyüme primi veriyor.
Yani burada mesele “ucuz mu?” değil.

Mesele şu:
Lumentum AI optik altyapı döngüsünün gerçek kazananlarından biri olabilir mi?

Benim cevabım: Evet, olabilir.

Ama bu hikâye yüksek beklenti taşıdığı için kademeli ve sabırlı olmak gerekir.
$LITE benim gözümde AI altyapı sepetinde izlenmesi gereken en önemli optik oyunculardan biri.

Yatırım tavsiyesi değildir.

#LITE $AAOI #AI #YapayZeka #Optics #Semiconductor #Borsa #Nasdaq #Yatırım

🌌📡 THE BLUEPRINT OF TOMORROW 📡🌌

On the world of Aurora-7, where intelligence flows like rivers of light and every street becomes part of a living network, Medusa Technologies unveils the Smart Street BioPhoton Fiber™ Corridor Blueprint.

Beneath every avenue, boulevard, industrial park, airport, and smart city district lies a new kind of infrastructure.

Not copper.

Not fragmented systems.

But a unified BioPhoton Fiber™ corridor engineered to become the nervous system of the intelligent city.

⚡ Powered by Medusa Batteries
⚙️ Stabilized by Medusa Flywheel Technology
🧠 Guided by the Quad Stack Architecture

Every meter of infrastructure is designed to communicate, monitor, optimize, and evolve.

The corridor senses traffic patterns.

It detects utility leaks.

It monitors environmental conditions.

It coordinates smart transportation systems.

It supports AI operations at city scale.

At the center of the blueprint stands the Aurora Core, where advanced materials, photonic engineering, graphene armor systems, and intelligent sensing layers converge into one continuous network of light.

Kid Nebula Gold charts the future.

Aurora Gold powers the foundation.

Medusa Technologies builds the bridge between infrastructure and intelligence.

From Earth to Aurora-7.

From city streets to planetary networks.

One Fiber.
One Nervous System.
One Intelligent Future.

🐍 Medusa Technologies
🌟 Kid Nebula Gold

Beneath every avenue, boulevard, industrial park, airport, and smart city district lies a new kind of infrastructure. Not copper. Not fragmented systems. But a unified BioPhoton Fiber™ corridor engineered to become the nervous system of the intelligent city. ⚡ Powered by Medusa Batteries ⚙️ Stabilized by Medusa Flywheel Technology 🧠 Guided by the Quad Stack Architecture Every meter of infrastructure is designed to communicate, monitor, optimize, and evolve. The corridor senses traffic patterns. It detects utility leaks. It monitors environmental conditions. It coordinates smart transportation systems. It supports AI operations at city scale. At the center of the blueprint stands the Aurora Core, where advanced materials, photonic engineering, graphene armor systems, and intelligent sensing layers converge into one continuous network of light. Kid Nebula Gold charts the future. Aurora Gold powers the foundation. Medusa Technologies builds the bridge between infrastructure and intelligence. From Earth to Aurora-7. $SIVE isn’t done. Not even close. 

We’re still in the very early innings of the AI infrastructure buildout and this one is rerating hard from microcap obscurity to the next big photonics winner.

I went in heavy months ago when it was flying under the radar. Now after the 8x run it’s still my largest position.

Dips are buying opportunities here, not signals to sell.

I’ll be adding more on any meaningful pullback. See you at the next leg up. This could be life changing money for those who hold conviction.

text:
If you have been paying attention to my recent themes around 800V DC, CPO, photonics, InP, SiC and GaN…

The idea is not just buying random AI suppliers.

The idea is investing around $NVDA, America’s national champion in AI, and the critical supply chains needed to support it.

A lot of these technologies are difficult.

Yields are hard.

Substrate supply is tight.

Laser capacity can become a bottleneck.

Export controls can create pressure.

Power architecture is becoming more complex.

And yes, bears can always point to those difficulties and say Nvidia’s supply chain should be shorted.

But I think that misses the bigger picture.

The point of investing in these critical companies is to help fund the capex, capacity, yield improvement and upstream supply needed to make the impossible become scalable.

More FAU / laser capacity.

More InP and red phosphorus substrate capacity.

More SiC and GaN power capacity.

More Western photonics and semiconductor infrastructure.

That is how difficult technology becomes real industrial capacity.

This is not just an AI trade.

This is a Western supply-chain buildout trade.

And at the center of it is $NVDA.

I still believe Nvidia is the platform.

I still believe the supply chain around Nvidia matters more than the market understands.

And honestly, I believe in Jensen.

text:
The path from raw inputs to AI end-user value is long and constantly mutating. Every layer has its own supply-demand curve with mid-cycle attempts at reinvention (e.g. grid power > on-site turbines, copper > photonics, HBM > next-gen memory stacking).

Yes, the aggregate AI infra curve is steep. But alpha lives in the component curves: the companies, their individual dynamics, how they're financed, where the market still underprices forward demand…

There's no grand unified theory. But two axioms apply to each sub-market:

1. Cost of capital is a thing. You create equity value when returns on invested capital exceed financing costs. Growth funded below that hurdle destroys value, no matter how big the TAM. That's the gravitational force that pulls us back down from lofty "revenue multiples."

2. Cyclicality is a thing. The second the market senses overshoot, multiples collapse — and you can be left painfully holding the bag.

But combing through this tangle is the fun part.

Yes, the aggregate AI infra", "id_str": "2065115073270743091", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 1, "retweet_count": 0, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "29890630"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNjUxMTUwNzMxNzg0ODA2NDA=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "The path from raw inputs to AI end-user value is long and constantly mutating. Every layer has its own supply-demand curve with mid-cycle attempts at reinvention (e.g. grid power > on-site turbines, copper > photonics, HBM > next-gen memory stacking). Yes, the aggregate AI infra curve is steep. But alpha lives in the component curves: the companies, their individual dynamics, how they're financed, where the market still underprices forward demand… There's no grand unified theory. But two axioms apply to each sub-market: 1. Cost of capital is a thing. You create equity value when returns on invested capital exceed financing costs. Growth funded below that hurdle destroys value, no matter how big the TAM. That's the gravitational force that pulls us back down from lofty "revenue multiples." 2. Cyclicality is a thing. The second the market senses overshoot, multiples collapse — and you can be left painfully holding the bag. But combing through this tangle is the fun part."}}}, "rest_id": "2065115073270743091", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "123", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
#TechPaperThursday #OpenAccess #Article | Theoretical analysis of fiber coupling and image transmission for flexible micro-LED near-eye displays

Researchers  developed a 3D model to analyze coupling efficiency and crosstalk between micro-LEDs and fiber image bundles for near-eye displays (NEDs).

Using #LightTools simulations and experimental validation, the study identified key design factors—including alignment, fiber NA, and micro-LED geometry—that can significantly improve image quality and efficiency in flexible AR/NED systems.

Read the full paper: https://t.co/nI4gJsC8gh

Figure 3: Hao Chen et al, https://t.co/RufG4tmvgQ
